narrative: As a Black male applicant with an upper-middle-class family background and plans to attend law school full-time, your profile stood out in several ways, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90 and LSAT score of 31. The admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on students' past academic and background details, estimated an 82.3% chance that you would fail it. This high predicted risk of failure is why your application was rejected—law schools use this to prioritize applicants likely to succeed and pass the bar promptly. The model's decision hinged most strongly on your Year 3 school decile ranking of 2, which placed you in the bottom 20% of your peers and significantly pushed the prediction toward expecting bar failure, as lower rankings like yours often correlate with struggles in rigorous legal training. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile of 2—again in the bottom 20%, well below the typical student's mid-range standing—which reinforced the model's concern about early academic challenges carrying forward. Your undergraduate GPA offered a slight counterbalance, nudging the prediction a bit toward passing since 2.90 is not far off the average of 3.21, but it wasn't strong enough to overcome the dominant impact of those decile rankings. Compared to typical students, who land around the 5th or 6th decile in both early and later years with higher LSAT averages near 37, your position in the lower tail of the distribution amplified the model's worries about bar exam readiness. While factors like your full-time status aligned with the vast majority of successful applicants, they didn't sway the outcome here. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—it's the model highlighting patterns from thousands of past students where early and midterm grades like yours predicted bar hurdles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score closer to average, strengthening your GPA through post-bac courses or a master's program, and seeking targeted academic advising to improve your class rankings in future endeavors. Many applicants in similar situations have succeeded by addressing these exact gaps, and reapplying with bolstered credentials could shift your predicted odds dramatically. Your determination as a Black man pursuing law already sets you apart—keep pushing, and the data will start working in your favor.
